Catalog Description

Programming I. (3-2). Credit 4. Basic concepts in using computation to enhance

problem solving abilities; understanding how people communicate with computers,

and how computing affects society; computational thinking; representation of data;

analysis of program behavior; methods for identifying and fixing errors in programs;

understanding abilities and limitation of programs; development and execution of

programs.

Additional Course Details

This is an introductory course designed for any student interested in using

computation to enhance their problem-solving abilities. No prior experience in

programming is necessary. Students will use their problem-solving abilities to

implement programs in Python.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Develop Computational Thinking: The learner can apply concepts in

computation to enhance problemp-solving abilities across various disciplines.

Implement Python Programs: The learner can design, write, and execute

functional programs in the Python programming language.

Understand Data Representation: The learner can comprehend how data is

internally represented within a computer and how people communicate with

computation to enhance problemp-solving abilities across various disciplines.

Implement Python Programs: The learner can design, write, and execute

functional programs in the Python programming language.

Understand Data Representation: The learner can comprehend how data is

internally represented within a computer and how people communicate with

computing systems.

Degug and Analyze Behavior: The learner can identify and fix errors while

analyzing the behavior and limitations of specific programs.

Special Course Designation

None

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Programming assignments and homeworks should be submitted on or before the due

dates. Programming assignment and homework grades will lose 25% for each day

after the due dates.

List of Topics:

Introduction to Python  1 week

Variables and Expressions 2 weeks

Types, Branches, and Loops 1 week

Functions, Recursion and Strings 3 weeks

Lists, Tuples, Sets, and Dictionaries 2 weeks

Classes 2 weeks

Exceptions 1 week

Modules and Files 1 week

Inheritance 1 week

Additional Course Information

Assignments will require use of standard programming style conventions

and documentation which will be illustrated in lab and lecture examples.

AI Statement

According to the Texas A&M University Definitions of Academic

Misconduct, plagiarism is the appropriation of another person's ideas,

processes, results or words without giving appropriate credit

(

). You should credit your use of anyone else's

words, graphic images, or ideas using standard citation styles. Artificial

Intelligence (AI) text generators and natural language processing tools

(colloquially, chatbots - such as ChatGPT), audio, computer code, video,

and image generators should not be used for any work for this class

without explicit permission of the instructor and appropriate attribution.

This includes, but is not limited to,

i. Creating or revising drafts

ii. Editing your work

iii. Reviewing a peer's work

aggiehonor.tamu.edu

This excludes pre-existing software additions such as spelling and

grammar checkers, which are acceptable.

Course Specific Attendance Policy

Attendance at all lectures is required to ensure that each student understands the

informational content of the class and becomes aware of any changes to the tentative

class exam, quiz, and assignment schedules as noted later in this document. Absence

from a lecture is not a valid excuse to be misinformed about any class activity, and

the student maintains the responsibility for all course content.
